metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the credential-source claim: `JIRA_URL` and `JIRA_USER` must be mapped from `vars.*` (repo Variables) not `secrets.*`, and only `JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*` — update What §1, AC1, and AC3 to match the pattern in `reconcile-bridge.yml` lines 87–89.
findings.finding: The plan states that `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` should be mapped 'from `secrets.*`' into the external job env, but `reconcile-bridge.yml` (the template the plan says to mirror) sources `JIRA_URL` and `JIRA_USER` from `vars.*` (repo Variables) and only `JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`. If the implementer follows the plan literally and uses `secrets.JIRA_URL` / `secrets.JIRA_USER`, the values will be empty unless the operator configures them as secrets instead of variables — breaking the live Jira gate. The operator-attested AC also calls all three 'secrets', compounding the confusion.
findings.location: What § item 1 ('map `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`'); AC line 1 ('JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN secrets'); AC line 3 (operator-attested, 'secrets')
findings.suggested_fix: In What §1: 'map `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER` from `vars.*` and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`'. In AC1 and AC3: replace 'secrets' with 'repo Variables (JIRA_URL, JIRA_USER) and secret (JIRA_API_TOKEN)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct `secrets.JIRA_URL` / `secrets.JIRA_USER` references in What §1 and AC bullet 1/3 to `vars.JIRA_URL` / `vars.JIRA_USER` (matching the repo convention in `reconcile-bridge.yml`); only `JIRA_API_TOKEN` should come from `secrets.*`.
findings.finding: The plan maps JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER from `secrets.*` but the repo's established convention in `reconcile-bridge.yml` sources them from `vars.JIRA_URL` / `vars.JIRA_USER` (GitHub Actions repository variables, not secrets); only `JIRA_API_TOKEN` is a secret. The AC and the operator-attest AC bullet also treat all three as 'secrets'. If JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER are stored as `vars.*` (the established pattern), the plan's `secrets.JIRA_URL` / `secrets.JIRA_USER` references would silently resolve to empty strings.
findings.location: What §1 ('map `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`'); AC bullet 1 ('maps `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN`'); AC bullet 3 ('the `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` secrets')
findings.suggested_fix: In the workflow edit, use `JIRA_URL: ${{ vars.JIRA_URL }}`, `JIRA_USER: ${{ vars.JIRA_USER }}`, `JIRA_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}` — mirroring reconcile-bridge.yml lines 87–89. Update AC bullet 3 to say '`JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER` repo variables and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` secret'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the `acli jira auth login` authentication step (mirroring `reconcile-bridge.yml` lines 174–185) to What §1 and AC bullet 1, so the plan specifies both installing acli and authenticating it before the pytest run.
findings.finding: The plan says to reuse the 'download+verify+install step' for acli from `reconcile-bridge.yml` but omits the companion 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step (reconcile-bridge.yml lines 174–185: `acli jira auth login --site … --email … --token`). Without authentication, `shutil.which('acli')` resolves to a binary (so `_live_jira_ready()` returns True and tests no longer skip), but every actual `acli` subprocess invocation fails at the auth layer. The plan's What §1 and AC bullet 1 only say 'installs the `acli` binary onto PATH' with no mention of the auth step.
findings.location: What §1 ('INSTALL the `acli` binary … adding `~/.acli` to PATH'); AC bullet 1 ('installs the `acli` binary onto PATH')
findings.suggested_fix: After the install step, add: `echo "$JIRA_API_TOKEN" | acli jira auth login --site "$JIRA_URL" --email "$JIRA_USER" --token` (mirroring reconcile-bridge.yml:180–185). Document this as a required step in the plan's What §1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the workflow mapping so JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER are read from `vars.*` (repository Variables) and only JIRA_API_TOKEN from `secrets.*`, matching the established reconcile-bridge.yml pattern (lines 87-89).
findings.finding: The plan says to map JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER 'from secrets.*' into the workflow env, but the established codebase pattern (reconcile-bridge.yml lines 87-88, 176-177, 209-210) stores JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER as repository VARIABLES (vars.*), not secrets. If the implementer follows the plan's 'secrets.*' wording, these two env vars will be empty on any repo where JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER are correctly stored as variables, silently preserving the all-skip condition the plan is meant to fix.
findings.location: What section, item 1: 'map JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.*'
findings.suggested_fix: In external-integration.yml, use `JIRA_URL: ${{ vars.JIRA_URL }}`, `JIRA_USER: ${{ vars.JIRA_USER }}`, and `JIRA_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}`; update AC1 and AC3 to reflect the vars/secrets split.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the acli workflow steps to also include the `acli jira auth login` authentication step (analogous to reconcile-bridge.yml lines 174-185), not just the download+verify+install step, so the binary is authenticated and `search_issues`-style calls succeed.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'reusing the established download+verify+install step from reconcile-bridge.yml' for acli, but reconcile-bridge.yml also has a distinct 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step (lines 174-185) that runs `acli jira auth login` to persist session credentials. test_verified_fake_contract_live.py (line 15-17) explicitly notes that search_issues 'additionally needs an authenticated acli, not merely the binary on PATH.' Omitting the auth step leaves acli installed but unauthenticated, so any external test that shells out to acli (search_issues in test_verified_fake_contract_live.py) will still skip or error even with all env vars set.
findings.location: What section, item 1: acli install description
findings.suggested_fix: After the acli install step, add a step: `echo "$JIRA_API_TOKEN" | acli jira auth login --site "$JIRA_URL" --email "$JIRA_USER" --token` with the same env mapping, mirroring reconcile-bridge.yml lines 174-185.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER credential sourcing with the established repo convention: source them as `vars.JIRA_URL` / `vars.JIRA_USER` (not `secrets.*`), matching reconcile-bridge.yml, and update AC3's 'secrets' wording accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan maps JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER from `secrets.*` but the repo's established prior art (reconcile-bridge.yml) sources them as `vars.*` (non-secret GitHub Actions repository variables), reserving `secrets.*` only for JIRA_API_TOKEN. Deviating from this convention without justification forces operators to provision JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER in both vars (for reconcile-bridge) and secrets (for external-integration), and the AC3 'operator-attested' item also calls all three 'secrets', reinforcing the divergence.
findings.location: What section, item 1 ('map JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.*') and AC line 3 ('JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN secrets')
findings.suggested_fix: In the What §1 description and AC3 wording, use `${{ vars.JIRA_URL }}` and `${{ vars.JIRA_USER }}` (repository variables) while keeping `${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}` as a secret — exactly mirroring reconcile-bridge.yml lines 87–89.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in What §1 and AC line 1/3 whether `JIRA_URL` and `JIRA_USER` come from `vars.*` (consistent with `reconcile-bridge.yml`) or `secrets.*`, and ensure the config-as-artifact test and operator-attested AC use the same store designation consistently.
findings.finding: The plan says to map `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`, but the established pattern in `reconcile-bridge.yml` maps non-sensitive `JIRA_URL` and `JIRA_USER` from `vars.*` (repo variables) and only the token from `secrets.*`. This inconsistency leaves the implementation and the operator-attested AC ambiguous about which GitHub store (Variables vs. Secrets) to use, risks the config-as-artifact test asserting the wrong binding, and unnecessarily obscures non-sensitive values in the secrets store.
findings.location: What §1 / AC line 1 / AC line 3 (operator-attested)
findings.suggested_fix: Align with `reconcile-bridge.yml`: map `JIRA_URL` and `JIRA_USER` from `vars.*` and only `JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`. Update the plan text in What §1 to read: 'map `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER` from `vars.*` and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`'; update AC line 1 and the operator-attested AC line 3 to distinguish repo Variables from repo Secrets accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan to map `JIRA_URL` and `JIRA_USER` from `vars.*` (not `secrets.*`), matching the convention in reconcile-bridge.yml and jira-sync-setup.md; only `JIRA_API_TOKEN` should come from `secrets.*`.
findings.finding: The plan maps JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER from `secrets.*` ('map `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`'), but the established repo convention (reconcile-bridge.yml, jira-sync-setup.md) stores JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER as GitHub repo *Variables* (vars.*), not secrets. If the operator follows the existing documentation to configure these as Variables, the `secrets.JIRA_URL` and `secrets.JIRA_USER` references will resolve to empty strings, causing silent auth failure.
findings.location: What > Item 1: 'map `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER`/`JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*` into the external job's env'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section and in the workflow YAML, use `JIRA_URL: ${{ vars.JIRA_URL }}`, `JIRA_USER: ${{ vars.JIRA_USER }}`, `JIRA_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}` — mirroring reconcile-bridge.yml lines 87-89 and 176-178.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the credential classification: source `JIRA_URL` and `JIRA_USER` from `vars.*` (GitHub Actions repository variables, matching the established `reconcile-bridge.yml` pattern) and only `JIRA_API_TOKEN` from `secrets.*`; update AC bullet 3 accordingly so the operator attestation refers to 'the `JIRA_URL`/`JIRA_USER` repo *variables* and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` *secret*'.
findings.finding: The plan maps JIRA_URL, JIRA_USER, AND JIRA_API_TOKEN all from secrets.*, but JIRA_URL (a plain URL) and JIRA_USER (an email address) are non-sensitive and the repo's established pattern in reconcile-bridge.yml sources them from vars.JIRA_URL / vars.JIRA_USER (GitHub Actions repository variables), reserving secrets.* only for JIRA_API_TOKEN. Treating non-sensitive values as secrets violates least-privilege (over-classification), masks non-sensitive data in logs unnecessarily, and propagates the miscategorization into the operator-attested AC which says these 'secrets' must be configured in GitHub.
findings.location: What section item 1 ('map JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.*') and AC bullet 3 (operator-attested: 'JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN secrets … are configured in the GitHub repo')
findings.suggested_fix: In What §1, change 'map JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.*' to 'map JIRA_URL from vars.JIRA_URL, JIRA_USER from vars.JIRA_USER, and JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN — matching the established convention in reconcile-bridge.yml'. In AC bullet 3, change 'JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN secrets' to 'JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER repo variables and JIRA_API_TOKEN secret'.

findings.finding: The plan proposes mapping JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER from secrets.* but the existing repo workflow (reconcile-bridge.yml) stores them as vars.* (GitHub Actions repository variables, non-secret), with only JIRA_API_TOKEN as a secret. If the external-integration.yml implementation uses secrets.JIRA_URL and secrets.JIRA_USER while the values are registered as vars, the expressions will silently resolve to empty strings, causing the Jira tests to skip and the canary to remain a false green.
findings.location: What section, item 1 / Acceptance Criteria first bullet: 'maps JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets into the external job env'
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror reconcile-bridge.yml: use `vars.JIRA_URL` and `vars.JIRA_USER` (non-sensitive) plus `secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN` (sensitive). Update the plan's wording to 'map JIRA_URL / JIRA_USER from vars.* and JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.*', and update the config-as-artifact test to assert the correct expression forms.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an `acli jira auth login --site $JIRA_URL --email $JIRA_USER --token` step (mirroring reconcile-bridge.yml lines 174-185) to the external-integration.yml job, after the binary install and before running pytest — acli reads auth from its config file, not environment variables.
findings.finding: The plan omits the `acli jira auth login` authentication step that reconcile-bridge.yml performs immediately after the binary install. The production AcliClient explicitly documents that 'ACLI does not read env vars for auth' and 'ACLI Go reads auth from its config file (set by `acli auth login`)' — so without this step, every acli subprocess call (create_issue, set_relationship, delete_issue) will fail with authentication errors even when JIRA_* env vars are set.
findings.location: What > Item 1: 'INSTALL the `acli` binary … adding `~/.acli` to PATH so `shutil.which("acli")` resolves'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step identical to reconcile-bridge.yml's 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' step (lines 174-185), mapping JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER from vars.* and JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN. Include this step in the What section's description of the workflow changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in What §1 and/or the config-as-artifact AC that `ACLI_SHA256` must be non-empty (the install step should `exit 1` if unset, not just warn), or explicitly acknowledge that sha256 verification is best-effort when the variable is absent.
findings.finding: The plan claims the reused acli install step is 'sha256-checked', but the step in `reconcile-bridge.yml` (lines 156–159) only emits a `::warning::` and continues if `ACLI_SHA256` is unset — it does NOT fail. If `ACLI_SHA256` is not provisioned as a repo variable, the scheduled canary silently installs an unverified binary, undermining the supply-chain integrity the plan intends.
findings.location: What §1 / AC line 1
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's What §1, change 'sha256-checked' to 'sha256-checked (ACLI_SHA256 must be set; the step fails if absent)' and add a pre-flight check analogous to the ACLI_VERSION guard at reconcile-bridge.yml lines 150–153 that exits non-zero when ACLI_SHA256 is unset.

findings.finding: The plan maps JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER from secrets.* but the established repo convention (reconcile-bridge.yml) stores them as vars.* (non-secret GitHub Actions repository variables), leaving the intended access classification undeclared and inconsistent. JIRA_URL and JIRA_USER are non-sensitive config values (a URL and a username/email); treating them as secrets over-classifies them, masks them unnecessarily in CI logs, and pollutes the secrets namespace — reducing the meaningful signal that secrets.* provides for genuinely sensitive values like JIRA_API_TOKEN.
findings.location: What → item 1 / Acceptance Criteria → first bullet: 'maps JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets'
findings.suggested_fix: In external-integration.yml, map JIRA_URL: ${{ vars.JIRA_URL }} and JIRA_USER: ${{ vars.JIRA_USER }} (matching reconcile-bridge.yml), and only JIRA_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}. Update the AC bullet to read 'maps JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER from vars.* and JIRA_API_TOKEN from secrets.*'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the PATH mechanism description: the `reconcile-bridge.yml` step installs acli via `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` (not by appending `~/.acli` to `PATH`) — update What §1 to accurately describe the symlink-to-`/usr/local/bin` approach (or explicitly state `$GITHUB_PATH` if a different approach is intended).
findings.finding: The plan says the acli PATH step works by 'adding `~/.acli` to `PATH`', but the actual `reconcile-bridge.yml` step the plan instructs to reuse installs acli by symlinking it into `/usr/local/bin` (`sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli`), not by appending `~/.acli` to `PATH`. An implementer following the plan's description may add `~/.acli` to `$GITHUB_PATH` or to the shell `PATH` directly, producing a different (and possibly broken) setup, and the config-as-artifact test that 'asserts … installs `acli` / puts it on PATH' may miss the difference.
findings.location: What § item 1 ('adding `~/.acli` to `PATH` so `shutil.which("acli")` resolves')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `test.yml`'s `external` job as a consumer of the conftest fail-on-all-skip hook and specify how to keep it passing (e.g., the hook only fires when a specific env var set by the dedicated `external-integration.yml` is present, or the `test.yml` job is excluded from the hook's scope).
findings.finding: The `test.yml` workflow has an `external` job (lines 348–383) that sets `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL=1` but has ALL credential env vars commented out — by design, so every external test self-skips and the job goes green. The plan's conftest fail-on-all-skip hook fires when `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL` is truthy AND zero external tests executed. After the hook is added, this `test.yml` external job would turn RED on every push/dispatch (all tests skip = canary trips). This consumer is not enumerated or acknowledged in the plan.
findings.location: What §2 (fail-on-all-skip canary); Scope (tests/external/conftest.py)
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a second env guard (e.g., `REBAR_CANARY_STRICT=1`) set only in `external-integration.yml`'s `external` job, and have the hook require BOTH `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL=1` AND `REBAR_CANARY_STRICT=1` before failing on all-skip. Alternatively, update test.yml's comment to acknowledge the hook and suppress it explicitly.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge that the acli install step cannot be truly 'reused' from `reconcile-bridge.yml` without a composite action or reusable workflow, and either (a) extract it into a composite action referenced by both, or (b) explicitly document that both workflows must be updated in lockstep and add a config-as-artifact test that asserts both use the same install logic.
findings.finding: The acli install step is described as 'reusing' the step from `reconcile-bridge.yml`, but GitHub Actions has no native step-reuse mechanism — this means copy-paste duplication. If the install procedure, download URL shape, or verification logic changes in `reconcile-bridge.yml`, `external-integration.yml` will silently diverge, breaking acli availability for the scheduled canary.
findings.location: What / item 1: '...reusing the established download+verify+install step from reconcile-bridge.yml'
findings.suggested_fix: Extract the acli download+verify+install block into a `.github/actions/install-acli/action.yml` composite action parameterised by `acli_version` and `acli_sha256`, then reference it with `uses: ./.github/actions/install-acli` in both `reconcile-bridge.yml` and `external-integration.yml`. The existing config-as-artifact test can then assert both workflows reference the same composite action.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or a sub-bullet to AC3) requiring evidence from at least one successful manual `workflow_dispatch` trigger after secrets are provisioned, showing the workflow log with ≥1 external test executed (not all-skipped), as the in-production proof that the new code path is functional.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion requires an actual end-to-end live trigger of the workflow (manual `workflow_dispatch` or waiting for the first scheduled run) to verify that the new code path — secrets mapped, acli installed, live Jira tests executed rather than skipped — works as deployed; the plan's offline tests and operator attestation do not exercise this path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: 'Operator also posts a link to a successful `workflow_dispatch` run log showing ≥1 external test executed (canary did not fire) as proof that the new code path is functional end-to-end.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a transient-failure handling strategy for the scheduled run — e.g., a job-level `continue-on-error: false` with a documented 're-run on transient failure' runbook, a step-level retry (e.g., `pytest-retry` or a shell retry wrapper), or a timeout + explicit skip on connection-error — so operators can distinguish infra outages from genuine drift.
findings.finding: The scheduled weekly run makes live Jira API calls (and LLM calls) against real external systems. No retry, backoff, or transient-failure tolerance is specified at the job or step level. A transient Jira/API outage during the weekly window will fire the canary red for reasons unrelated to code drift, eroding operator trust in the signal.
findings.location: What / item 1 (schedule trigger) and AC item 1; Why section (live mutations description)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete default cron expression in the plan (e.g., `'0 6 * * 1'` — Mondays 06:00 UTC) so the executor and the config-as-artifact test both have an unambiguous value to pin.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a 'weekly' cron trigger but omits the concrete cron expression, leaving the executor to choose an arbitrary day/time with no stated default.
findings.location: What §1 — 'add an on.schedule cron trigger (weekly)'
findings.suggested_fix: In What §1, replace 'weekly' with the explicit expression: `cron: '0 6 * * 1'`; update AC1 to assert that exact string in the config-as-artifact test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the mechanism the hook uses to identify 'external tests' (e.g., 'all items collected in this conftest's directory subtree' or 'items bearing a `@pytest.mark.external` marker') so the behavioral test and the hook implementation are unambiguously aligned.
findings.finding: The conftest fail-on-all-skip hook must decide which collected tests count as 'external' for the zero-executed check, but the plan does not specify the identification mechanism (all tests collected in the session, tests under `tests/external/`, a pytest marker, or another criterion).
findings.location: What §2 — 'FAILS the session if EVERY external test skipped (zero executed)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to What §2: 'The hook counts only items whose `nodeid` is rooted under `tests/external/` (i.e., all items visible to this conftest); no additional marker is required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in What §1 (and cover in AC §1) that the scheduled job must set `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL=1` (e.g., as a job-level `env:` entry), so the conftest canary hook activates on scheduled runs.
findings.finding: What item 1 adds an `on.schedule` trigger but never states the scheduled job must also set `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL=1`. The conftest canary hook (What item 2) only fires when `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL` is truthy. If the scheduled job inherits the workflow's existing env config unchanged, the hook is silently a no-op on scheduled runs — the exact false-green the plan exists to prevent.
findings.location: What §1 (workflow changes) vs What §2 (conftest hook guard) vs Why (problem statement)
findings.suggested_fix: In the workflow, add `REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL: '1'` to the external job's `env:` block (or as a step-level env on the pytest invocation) for both the `workflow_dispatch` and the new `on.schedule` triggers. Reference this in What §1 and add a pin assertion in the config-as-artifact test (AC §1).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the plan for placing the fail-on-all-skip check in the conftest session hook rather than a workflow shell step (e.g. codebase alignment with the existing pytest_sessionfinish pattern in tests/conftest.py, testability via the behavioral unit test).
findings.finding: The plan states what mechanism to use (conftest session hook, schedule trigger, acli install) but provides no positive rationale for why a pytest session hook in conftest.py was chosen over a workflow-level approach (e.g. parsing --junit-xml output in a shell step) or a dedicated canary test module.
findings.location: What section, item 2 (fail-on-all-skip canary description)

findings.finding: The plan asserts the live Jira test 'targets a read-only / uniquely-tagged project (no mass mutation)', but reading test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py shows it calls client.create_issue() twice and client.delete_issue() in finally — it MUTATES the Jira project. There is no unique-tag enforcement; the project key defaults to 'DIG' with no isolation guard. The 'non-destructive' claim rests on cleanup running, not on read-only access.
findings.location: Plan section 'What', item 1: 'Keep it non-destructive: the live test targets a read-only / uniquely-tagged project (no mass mutation).'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'targets a read-only / uniquely-tagged project (no mass mutation)' with: 'creates and deletes probe issues in a dedicated sandboxed Jira project (passed via a `JIRA_PROJECT` repository secret/variable); the workflow should map `JIRA_PROJECT` alongside the other Jira secrets so the scheduled run is isolated from any production project.'

findings.finding: The plan maps JIRA_* credentials into external-integration.yml but does not install the `acli` binary, which all live Jira tests require (via `shutil.which('acli') is not None` in every `_live_jira_ready()` gate). On the scheduled run, even with secrets correctly configured, every Jira test will skip with 'no live Jira creds / acli binary' — triggering the all-skip canary as a false positive and making the canary permanently RED on a correctly-wired setup.
findings.location: What §1 / external-integration.yml schedule job / Acceptance Criteria AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Copy the 'Cache acli binary', 'Download + verify + install acli', and 'Add acli to PATH' steps from `.github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml` (lines 136-185) into the `external` job in `external-integration.yml`, referencing the same `vars.ACLI_VERSION` / `vars.ACLI_SHA256` / `secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN` / `vars.JIRA_URL` / `vars.JIRA_USER` variables. The acli auth login step should follow the credential-mapping env block so the binary is both present and authenticated before pytest runs.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that mapping JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN into the workflow env is sufficient for the live Jira test to authenticate and execute, but test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py::_live_jira_ready() also hard-gates on `shutil.which('acli')` (the Atlassian CLI binary). On a stock ubuntu-latest runner, `acli` is not pre-installed. With secrets configured but `acli` absent, the test still hard-skips — causing the proposed fail-on-all-skip canary to fire red on every scheduled run, generating persistent false alarms even after operator secret configuration.
findings.location: What § 1 ('Schedule + wire Jira secrets') and AC 'JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN … so the live Jira test can authenticate'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a workflow step (e.g., `sudo apt-get install -y acli` or a download+install from Atlassian's release endpoint) before the pytest run step in the `external` job. Mirror the pattern of the existing `Ensure jq is installed` step. Then update the AC operator-attested item to include `acli` binary availability as a prerequisite alongside the JIRA_* secrets.

findings.finding: The live Jira external tests gate on `shutil.which('acli') is not None` (verified in test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py:67, test_link_sync_live.py:36, test_reconcile_live.py:38, test_verified_fake_contract_live.py:47), but the plan adds only JIRA_* env vars and a cron trigger to external-integration.yml — it does not install the `acli` binary. Without acli on PATH, `_live_jira_ready()` returns False and all four Jira tests skip regardless of credentials, causing the all-skip canary to fire as a false alarm (or, if the canary distinguishes this case, the live Jira path is never exercised). The reconcile-bridge.yml workflow has a full acli download/verify/install step that serves as reference implementation.
findings.location: What / Scope — .github/workflows/external-integration.yml; also tests/external/test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py:67, test_link_sync_live.py:36, test_reconcile_live.py:38, test_verified_fake_contract_live.py:47
findings.suggested_fix: Copy the 'Cache acli binary', 'Download + verify + install acli', and 'Add acli to PATH and authenticate' steps from .github/workflows/reconcile-bridge.yml (lines ~136–183) into the `external` job in external-integration.yml, using `ACLI_VERSION` as a repo variable and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` for `acli jira auth login`. This ensures shutil.which('acli') resolves on the runner.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
